coredata.dc:title: A clean and sustainable method for recycling of lithium from spent lithium iron phosphate battery powder by using formic acid and oxygen
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tianyu
Mahandra, Harshit
Choi, Yeonuk
Li, Weilun
Zhang, Zhifei
Zhao, Zhongwei
Chen, Ailiang
coredata.dc:description: With the widespread adoption of lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries, the imperative recycling of LiFePO4 batteries waste presents formidable challenges in resource recovery, environmental preservation, and socio-economic advancement. Given the current overall lithium recovery rate in LiFePO4 batteries is below 1 %, there is a compelling demand for an eco-friendly, cost-efficient, and sustainable solution. This study introduces a green and sustainable recycling method that employs environmentally benign formic acid and readily available oxygen as reaction agents for selectively leaching lithium from discarded lithium iron phosphate powder. Formic acid was employed as the leaching agent, and oxygen served as the oxidizing agent. Utilizing a single-factor variable approach, various factors including formic acid concentration, oxygen flow rate, leaching time, liquid-to-solid ratio, and reaction temperature were individually investigated. Moreover, the feasibility of this method was explored mechanistically by analyzing E-pH diagrams of the Li-Fe-P-H2O system. Results demonstrate that under conditions of 2.5 mol/L formic acid concentration, 0.12 L/min oxygen flow rate, 25 mL/g liquid-to-solid ratio, 70 °C reaction temperature, and 3 h reaction time, lithium leaching efficiency exceeds 99.9 %, with iron leaching efficiency only at 1.7 %. Moreover, we also explored using air instead of oxygen as the oxidant and get the excellent lithium leaching rate (97.81 %) and low iron leaching rate (4.81 %), which shows the outstanding selectivity. Furthermore, the environmentally benign composition of the chemical reagents, comprising only C, H, and O elements, establishes it as a genuinely green and sustainable technology for secondary resource recovery. It can be considered as a highly environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and efficient approach. Nevertheless, in the current context of carbon neutrality and sustainable development, this method undoubtedly holds excellent prospects for industrialization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium
Selective leaching
Recovery
Recycling
Waste lithium-ion batteries
Lithium iron phosphate

Cost calculations and summary of oxygen and formic method

E-pH diagram of the Li-Fe-P-H2O system at 298.15 K (a) [Li] = [Fe] = [P] =10−1 M; (b) [Li] = [Fe] = [P] =10−3 M; (c) [Li] = [Fe] = [P] =10−5 M. data from (Jing et al., 2019).
The schematic diagram of the selective leaching reaction process.
Effect of reaction time oxidized by air.

Experimental conditions for reaction time varying.
Experimental conditions for oxygen rate varying.

Experimental conditions for solution volume varying.
Experimental conditions for air as the oxidant.

head.ce:title: A clean and sustainable method for recycling of lithium from spent lithium iron phosphate battery powder by using formic acid and oxygen

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Robert M. Buchan Department of Mining Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the widespread adoption of lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries, the imperative recycling of LiFePO4 batteries waste presents formidable challenges in resource recovery, environmental preservation, and socio-economic advancement. Given the current overall lithium recovery rate in LiFePO4 batteries is below 1 %, there is a compelling demand for an eco-friendly, cost-efficient, and sustainable solution. This study introduces a green and sustainable recycling method that employs environmentally benign formic acid and readily available oxygen as reaction agents for selectively leaching lithium from discarded lithium iron phosphate powder. Formic acid was employed as the leaching agent, and oxygen served as the oxidizing agent. Utilizing a single-factor variable approach, various factors including formic acid concentration, oxygen flow rate, leaching time, liquid-to-solid ratio, and reaction temperature were individually investigated. Moreover, the feasibility of this method was explored mechanistically by analyzing E-pH diagrams of the Li-Fe-P-H2O system. Results demonstrate that under conditions of 2.5 mol/L formic acid concentration, 0.12 L/min oxygen flow rate, 25 mL/g liquid-to-solid ratio, 70 °C reaction temperature, and 3 h reaction time, lithium leaching efficiency exceeds 99.9 %, with iron leaching efficiency only at 1.7 %. Moreover, we also explored using air instead of oxygen as the oxidant and get the excellent lithium leaching rate (97.81 %) and low iron leaching rate (4.81 %), which shows the outstanding selectivity. Furthermore, the environmentally benign composition of the chemical reagents, comprising only C, H, and O elements, establishes it as a genuinely green and sustainable technology for secondary resource recovery. It can be considered as a highly environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and efficient approach. Nevertheless, in the current context of carbon neutrality and sustainable development, this method undoubtedly holds excellent prospects for industrialization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries, as outstanding, efficient, and environmentally friendly energy storage materials, play a crucial role in various fields of today's society (Zhao et al., 2024; Cui et al., 2022; Ge et al., 2021). They are indispensable in applications such as electric vehicles/hybrid electric vehicles, portable electronic devices, battery energy storage systems, medical equipment, military devices, aerospace equipment, children's toys, and many other areas (Zhao et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2022; Bian et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020). The contributions of John B. Goodenough, Stanley Whittingham, and Akira Yoshino to the field of lithium-ion batteries were recognized with the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 (Belharouak and Passerini, 2020; O'Neill, 2020). This further underscores the societal importance and acknowledgment of lithium-ion batteries, highlighting their crucial role in human civilization and scientific technology.

Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries, considered excellent lithium-ion batteries, exhibit lower dependence on strategically scarce metals like nickel and cobalt compared to ternary lithium-ion batteries, lithium cobalt oxide batteries, and lithium manganese oxide batteries (Zhao et al., 2024; Kumawat et al., 2022). They require the same stoichiometric amount of lithium compared to other LIB chemistries, and abundant raw materials iron and critical raw material phosphorus present in the Earth's crust (Kumawat et al., 2022). This not only reduces production costs but also mitigates the environmental impact of elements like nickel and cobalt. Due to the toxic nature of nickel and cobalt to both humans and the environment, prolonged exposure in environments containing nickel and cobalt may reduce organ size/weight, impair cognitive functions, affect keratinization diseases and vascular complications, and even lead to cancer (Xue et al., 2021). Notably, nickel is considered an immunotoxic and carcinogenic substance, and prolonged exposure to nickel environments can result in conditions such as contact dermatitis, cardiovascular diseases, asthma, pulmonary fibrosis, and respiratory tract cancer. The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classifies soluble and insoluble nickel compounds as Group 1 carcinogens, and nickel and its alloys as Group 2B carcinogens (Genchi et al., 2020; Begum et al., 2022). Moreover, research has also confirmed the accumulation of cobalt in water bodies and farmlands, impacting biomass and reducing crop yields, causing significant economic losses to farmers (Mahey et al., 2020). In comparison, phosphorus and iron are relatively safer. As it is well-known, iron is an essential element in biological organisms, participating in crucial biochemical processes such as oxygen transport, energy production, DNA metabolism, and is therefore beneficial to human health in moderate amounts (Silvestri et al., 2023). While phosphorus leakage can lead to eutrophication of water bodies, causing excessive algae growth and biodiversity destruction, these phosphorus compounds can be recovered from waste streams and converted into valuable phosphorus fertilizers (Brownlie et al., 2021; Garske et al., 2020). Furthermore, LiFePO4 batteries are increasingly being utilized due to their long life span, high charging-discharging efficiency, high safety, reasonable charge-discharge voltage, excellent thermal stability, outstanding cycling performance, and low toxicity (Zhao et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2022; Gong et al., 2022; Fatima et al., 2022; Mauger and Julien, 2017). According to statistics, the market for lithium iron phosphate is growing at a rate of 3 % annually (statista, 2023). It is anticipated that by 2025, the global market for lithium iron phosphate will reach 64,000 tons (Forte et al., 2021). Especially in the electric vehicle sector, LiFePO4 batteries are gradually replacing other types of lithium-ion batteries and are extensively applied by groups such as Tesla, Volkswagen, Ford, and Daimler (Gong et al., 2022; Kumawat et al., 2022).

However, the lifespan of LiFePO4 batteries is limited, typically ranging from 5 to 8 years (Gong et al., 2022). This results in the prospect of a significant and increasing amount of LiFePO4 battery waste in the coming years (Wager and Hischier, 2015; Ma et al., 2021). If not properly managed, these batteries not only occupy land resources for discard or landfill, but also pose threats to the natural environment, including the atmosphere, water bodies, and soil (Forte et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Zhong et al., 2019; Bi et al., 2021). Although the hazards are relatively smaller compared to other types of lithium-ion batteries, there still exists the risk of combustion, explosion, and leakage, particularly concerning components like electrolytes, organic binder, separators, and plastic casings, which can pose fatal threats to the environment (Zhao et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2022). Additionally, the substantial presence of phosphorus in these batteries can lead to eutrophication of water bodies, disrupting ecological balance. Therefore, proper disposal is necessary, and recycling emerges as a sensible choice (Zhao et al., 2024; Gong et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Bi et al., 2021; Shentu et al., 2021).

Furthermore, recycling offers a way to turn waste into wealth, potentially yielding economic benefits (Wang et al., 2022; Steward et al., 2019). Due to the abundant presence of lithium, discarded LiFePO4 batteries represent a high-quality secondary resource. Recycling them can alleviate the current global imbalance and shortage of lithium resources. From a perspective of sustainable development, recycling is imperative (Zhang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023a; Zhao et al., 2023b). However, the overall recycling rate of lithium from spent LiFePO4 batteries is lower than 1 % (Zhao et al., 2024). To address this dilemma, proposing a new recycling method is inevitable.

Presently, for the recycling of spent LiFePO4 batteries, the hydrometallurgy method is a competitive approach due to its low cost, fast leaching speed, low energy consumption, small environmental impact, and high product purity (Zhao et al., 2024; Forte et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023c; Li et al., 2022). Selective leaching is an efficient and rational method for LiFePO4 recovery, widely studied and acknowledged for its high production efficiency and low cost (Fatima et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023b; Li et al., 2017; Jin et al., 2022). Moreover, from a sustainable development standpoint, using organic acids containing only carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen as leaching agents is more environmentally friendly. Unlike hydrochloric acid, these organic acids do not produce harmful chlorine gas and do not contain elements, such as nitric acid or phosphoric acid, that contribute to water eutrophication (Zhao et al., 2023a). Currently, organic acids such as formic acid (Mahandra and Ghahreman, 2021) and oxalic acid (Zhang et al., 2023) have been studied and achieved satisfactory results. In our previous research (Zhao et al., 2023b; Zhao et al., 2023c), we used formic acid and hydrogen peroxide as reaction reagents, conducted a series of studies, and achieved success. However, hydrogen peroxide is unstable, and improper storage conditions can lead to its degradation. Additionally, the reaction with hydrogen peroxide is vigorous, often resulting in splashing and frothing during production, and typically requires a larger reactor (more than ten times the slurry volume) to complete the leaching process, increasing safety hazards and production costs (Zhao et al., 2023b). Moreover, hydrogen peroxide actually undergoes side reactions during the reaction; it is catalytically decomposed by LiFePO4 powder, leading to a decrease in its utilization efficiency (Zhao et al., 2023b). Furthermore, hydrogen peroxide is harmful to the human body; it easily penetrates biological membranes and is absorbed through mucous membranes, skin, and lungs, leading to mechanical pressure damage to tissues or blood vessels (Abdollahi and Hosseini, 2014; Marshall et al., 1995). Therefore, it is necessary to choose a milder, more stable, and readily available oxidant to replace hydrogen peroxide.

To avoid the drawbacks associated with using hydrogen peroxide in traditional methods, it is necessary to develop a new method for selectively leaching lithium that can circumvent the disadvantages of hydrogen peroxide. This new method should achieve the selective leaching of lithium from discarded lithium iron phosphate under mild conditions, while ensuring both high lithium extraction rates and excellent selectivity. Therefore, we conducted experimental exploration using environmentally friendly raw materials, formic acid, and oxygen. Since oxygen is widely available in the air, it is cost-effective and easily accessible. Additionally, the reaction with oxygen is milder compared to hydrogen peroxide, eliminating the risk of vigorous reactions leading to overflowing and splashing, and it does not undergo catalytic decomposition. Moreover, the formic acid used in the experiment is the strongest acid among organic acids, with the highest acid density. Its acidity is tenfold that of acetic acid. Releasing a hydrogen ion, formic acid has the shortest carbon chain among organic acids, requiring the smallest mass and volume, making it highly efficient. Furthermore, formic acid only contains the elements C, H, and O, in contrast to common inorganic acids such as sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, and phosphoric acid, which may introduce eutrophication elements like N and P into water bodies. It also avoids the generation of waste gases and wastewater containing S and Cl. Formic acid is easily biodegradable, and its solvent's environmental risk level is reported to be 7 (high level indicating low risk), signifying low environmental risk and making it a very safe and clean reagent (Mahandra and Ghahreman, 2021). In summary, this method exhibits environmentally friendly characteristics, making it a sustainable approach. Normally, we consider a lithium leaching rate of 99.9 % or higher, and an iron leaching rate of around 1 % is excellent. And the lithium leaching rate of 95 % is acceptable. To achieve these core objectives, the following studies were conducted.

In the research process, we examined factors such as formic acid concentration, reaction time, oxygen rate, liquid-to-solid ratio, solution volume, reaction temperature, etc. Furthermore, we applied the distribution ratio and separation coefficient originally used for solvent extraction to selective leaching to visually measure its leaching efficiency and selectivity. Additionally, the study also investigated the substitution of air for oxygen.

The waste lithium iron phosphate powder was obtained after pretreatment from a local enterprise in Kingston, Ontario, Canada, containing 4.18 % lithium and 30.06 % iron. The Pure Compressed Oxygen was sourced from a company in Kingston, Canada, and formic acid was procured from Fisher in the United States, with a purity of 88 % (v/v).

Add a predetermined stoichiometric ratio of formic acid and deionized water to a reactor, and uniformly mix them by magnetic stirring. Then, introduce oxygen into the solution at a fixed rate and add lithium iron phosphate powder to the stirring solution to initiate the reaction. Explore the optimal experimental conditions by varying factors such as formic acid concentration, oxygen flow rate, reaction time, liquid-to-solid ratio, and reaction temperature in different experiments.

After reacting under specific experimental conditions for a designated time, the reaction slurry is filtered through vacuum filtration. The leaching residue is then washed with deionized water. The collected filtrate is mixed with the washing solution, and a small amount of nitric acid is added. The mixture is adjusted to a volume of 250 mL. The solutions obtained after adjusting the volume, diluting by a factor of 10, and diluting by a factor of 100 are used for the analysis of Li and Fe concentrations. The solid obtained from the reaction is placed in a vacuum drying oven at 110 °C for 8 h, taken out, re-ground, and then used for XRD analysis.

The concentrations of lithium ions and iron ions in the solution were analyzed using a 4200 MP-AES (Agilent Technologies) instrument. The specific procedure involved diluting each mixed solution by factors of 10 and 100, followed by analysis using the 4200 MP-AES instrument. Prior to and after the leaching process, X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was performed on both the waste lithium iron phosphate battery powder and residue.

The leaching efficiency calculation is outlined in Eq. (1). Additionally, to better reflect selectivity, the distribution ratio (D
1 ) and separation coefficient (β
M1/M2 ) were computed using Eqs. (2) and (3). Higher values of D
1 and β
M1/M2 indicate enhanced leaching selectivity for lithium.
(1)
w 1
% =
c 1
× V /
m ×
x 1
%
× 100 %
(2)
D 1
=
c 1
× V /
m ×
x 1
% −
c 1
×
V 1
(3)
β
M 1 / M 2
=
D 1
/
D 2
=
c 1
× V /
m ×
x 1
% −
c 1
×
V 1
/
c 2
× V /
m ×
x 2
% −
c 2
×
V 2

Here, M
1 and M
2 denote two distinct metals, where w
1 % represents the leaching efficiency of M
1 , D
1 is the distribution ratio of M1, and βM1/M2 is the separation coefficient between M
1 and M
2 (M
1 >M
2 ). c
1 and c
2 correspond to the concentrations of metal ions in the post-leaching solution (mixed with washing solution) of M
1 and M
2 , respectively. V stands for the post-leaching solution volume (mixed with washing solution), m is the initial mass of battery powder, and x
1 % and x
2 % respectively indicate the percentage of M
1 and M
2 in the initial battery powder.

Generally, for spent LiFePO4 batteries, a preprocessing step is essential before recycling. This process involves discharging, dismantling, shredding, crushing, grinding, etc. In this context, discharging process is employed to prevent potential fires or explosions during the dismantling of discarded LiFePO4 batteries (Forte et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022). Processes such as dismantling, shredding, crushing, and grinding are carried out to facilitate the powdering of battery materials, enabling rapid leaching reactions. Subsequently, a physical separation method is employed to isolate lithium iron phosphate from the mixed battery waste for leaching experiments (Zhao et al., 2024; Zhong et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2023c; Kumar et al., 2021). In practice, the preprocessing procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1 .
After the preprocessing, the main component of the powder is lithium iron phosphate, as indicated by the XRD analysis results shown in Fig. 2 .

The E-pH diagram for the Li-Fe-P-H2O system is shown in Fig. 3 . It can be observed that under different conditions of E and pH, Li, Fe, and P exist in different forms in either the solid or liquid phase. When E and pH values fall within the range of region (A), all three elements exist in the solid phase in the form of lithium iron phosphate. However, as the pH decreases and the E value appropriately increases, the stable range for Li, Fe, and P shifts from region (A) to region (B). In this case, divalent ferrous ions are oxidized to trivalent ferric ions, and due to the compound's overall valence being 0, lithium ions enter the solution. Mechanistically, the selective leaching method is feasible, and the chemical reaction equation is shown in Eq. (4). This process can be conducted under milder acidity, typically resulting in relatively low acid consumption.
(4)
LiFe
II
PO 4
s
→ Fe
III
PO 4
s
+
Li +
aq
+
e −

From Fig. 3, it can be seen that in regions (C) and (D), all ions enter the solution, leading to the complete leaching of all components in lithium iron phosphate. In comparison to the selective leaching method, this process usually requires very low pH values, indicating a need for more acid consumption to create a stronger acidic environment. The difference lies in the oxidation states of iron ions in the solution under different E values. High E values represent stronger oxidizing conditions, and ferrous ions in lithium iron phosphate will be oxidized to trivalent ferric ions. However, under the reducing conditions of low E values, ferrous ions have no chance to be oxidized to trivalent, continuing to exist in the solution as divalent ferrous ions. Specifically, the chemical reaction equations for these two processes are represented by Eq. (5) and Eq. (6).
(5)
LiFe
II
PO 4
s
+
3H +
→
Fe
3 +
aq
+
H 3
PO 4
aq
+
Li +
aq
+
e −
(6)
LiFe
II
PO 4
s
+
3H +
→
Fe
2 +
aq
+
H 3
PO 4
aq
+
Li +
aq

Therefore, acidity is a crucial factor in selective leaching, and maintaining an appropriate acidity is essential during the process. The acidity of the solution used should not be too strong; otherwise, regardless of the presence of oxidants, the overall structure of lithium iron phosphate will be disrupted, leading to the complete dissolution of all components into the solution.

In addition, by comparing Figs. 3 (a), (b), and (c), it is evident that, as various ion concentrations decrease, except for region (E), all graph shapes in other regions show a trend of moving to the right. The right edge line of region (B) gradually reaches a neutral or alkaline position. This implies that under neutral and alkaline conditions, selective leaching of lithium from lithium iron phosphate can also be achieved. However, the ion concentrations shown in the three graphs are 10−1 M, 10−3 M, and 10−5 M, respectively. These low concentrations are suitable only for laboratory research and are economically unfeasible and impractical in industrial applications. Conversely, if ion concentrations in the solution increase, these regions will shift to the left, causing the right edge line of region (B) to move leftward and be <7. Thus, the selective leaching process requires acid to provide an appropriate acidic environment. Furthermore, according to this pattern, the higher the ion concentration in the solution, the greater the acidity required. And higher concentrations are more realistic in industrial production. In summary, an acidic environment is often necessary in industrial production processes.

Here, we utilize formic acid as a leaching agent to provide an acidic environment for the reaction system. Oxygen is employed as an oxidant, playing a role in oxidizing divalent ferrous ions in lithium iron phosphate to trivalent ferric ions. This process enables the extraction of lithium ions from lithium iron phosphate into the solution. Moreover, phosphorus and iron still remain in the solid form as iron phosphate. The chemical equation for the reaction is illustrated in Eq. (7), and a schematic diagram of the reaction process is shown in Fig. 4 .
(7)
LiFe
II
PO 4
s
+ HCOOH
aq
+
O 2
g
→ Fe
III
PO 4
s
+
HCOO −
aq
+
Li +
aq
+
H 2
O
aq

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of formic acid concentration
ce:section.ce:para: During the leaching process, acidity is a crucial influencing factor, and generally, a stronger acidic environment is more favorable for the leaching procedure. Therefore, this study initially explores the impact of formic acid concentration on the leaching reaction, with experimental conditions outlined in Table 1 and results depicted in Fig. 5 .

Fig. 5 illustrated a positive correlation between the leaching rates of lithium and iron and the concentration of formic acid. Notably, the leaching rate of lithium is significantly higher than that of iron, indicating a preliminary selectivity of the system for the leaching of lithium and iron. The inconsistency in their variations suggests that enhancing the selective leaching of lithium is feasible by improving experimental conditions. From Fig. 5, it is evident that excessively low formic acid concentrations do not yield high lithium leaching rates. When the formic acid concentration reaches 2.5 mol/L, the lithium leaching rate exceeds 99.91 %, indicating nearly complete leaching of lithium. However, simultaneously, the iron leaching rate is 15.13 %, suggesting that, despite selective leaching, the selectivity is not ideal at this point. Further exploration of alternative experimental conditions is necessary.

Upon further increasing the formic acid concentration, the lithium leaching rate plateaus, while the iron leaching rate shows a significant increase. At this point, leaching selectivity deteriorates, which is evidently not the desired experimental outcome. In conclusion, it can be considered that a formic acid concentration of 2.5 mol/L is a reasonable experimental condition.

In addition to formic acid concentration, reaction time is also a crucial influencing factor. Here, reaction time affects the amount of oxygen introduced. Under a fixed oxygen flow rate, if the reaction time is too short, it may result in insufficient oxygen intake, leading to incomplete reactions. On the other hand, if the reaction time is too long, it can lead to a wasteful excess of oxygen, increasing costs. Therefore, exploring the impact of reaction time on leaching effectiveness is essential. The experimental conditions for this section are outlined in Table 2 , and the results are presented in Fig. 6 .

In this experiment, as the reaction time increases, the amount of oxygen introduced also gradually increases. Therefore, theoretically, a longer reaction time is advantageous for the leaching of lithium. Fig. 6 indicates a positive correlation between the leaching rate of lithium and the reaction time (amount of oxygen introduced), while the leaching rate of iron shows the opposite trend, decreasing with increasing reaction time. This experimental result also confirms the conclusion drawn from the analysis in Fig. 3, namely, that as the oxidative capacity increases, the ability to selectively leach lithium also enhances. When the reaction time reaches 3 h, the leaching rate of lithium rises to 99.92 %, indicating almost complete leaching of lithium. Therefore, a reaction time of 3 h can be considered reasonable.

Due to the current exploratory experiments not being conducted in a closed system and the limited ability of the aqueous solution to dissolve oxygen, oxygen is released into the air simultaneously with its introduction. In such a scenario, if the oxygen flow rate is too fast, it can result in a significant loss of oxygen and wastage. On the contrary, if the oxygen flow rate is too slow, it can lead to incomplete reactions. Therefore, setting a reasonable oxygen flow rate is also essential. The experimental conditions for exploring the oxygen flow rate are outlined in Table 3 , and the results are presented in Fig. 7 .

As shown in Fig. 7, the leaching rate of lithium increases with the increase in oxygen flow rate, reaching a maximum and then stabilizing. In contrast, the leaching rate of iron exhibits the opposite trend, with the two curves roughly symmetrically distributed. Notably, 0.12 L/min is the minimum flow rate to maintain a high lithium leaching rate, while the leaching rate of iron remains at a relatively low level. Furthermore, with the increase in oxygen flow rate, there is no significant change in the leaching rates of lithium and iron. Therefore, it is considered that 0.12 L/min is a reasonable oxygen flow rate.
This section explores the impact of the liquid-to-solid ratio, with experimental conditions outlined in Table 4 and results presented in Fig. 8 .

In Fig. 8, the leaching rates of iron and lithium both show an overall increasing trend with an increase in the liquid-to-solid ratio. When the liquid-to-solid ratio is too low, the amount of formic acid in the solution is relatively small, making it challenging to maintain a high leaching rate. Therefore, under low liquid-to-solid ratio conditions, it is often difficult to achieve satisfactory leaching results. On the other hand, when the liquid-to-solid ratio is too high, the leaching rate of lithium does not further increase. Instead, more iron is leached out, and the excessive liquid-to-solid ratio leads to an increase in the consumption of formic acid and deionized water. After leaching, the concentration of lithium ion in the solution decreases, resulting in more wastewater discharge. Clearly, an excessively high liquid-to-solid ratio is also unfavorable.
From Fig. 8, it can be observed that a liquid-to-solid ratio of 25 mL/g is the optimal experimental condition, at which the leaching rate of lithium just reaches its maximum.

In the previous section of the study, the ratio of leaching solution to lithium iron phosphate powder (liquid-to-solid ratio) was adjusted while ensuring the constant composition of the leaching solution. In this case, once the volume of the solution changes, the amount of acid consumed will also change accordingly. So, when the formic acid dosage remains constant, how does solely changing the solution volume affect the reaction?

To explore this question, this section, under the condition of a constant formic acid dosage (using the formic acid dosage when the liquid-to-solid ratio is 25 mL/g and the formic acid concentration is 2.5 mol/L, as this dosage can completely leach lithium from lithium iron phosphate powder), solely varied the amount of deionized water to adjust the solution volume and leach an equivalent mass of battery powder. The experimental conditions are outlined in Table 5 , and the results are presented in Fig. 9 .

Although the amount of formic acid is sufficient to leach all the lithium from the battery powder, the leaching rate of lithium is affected when the solution volume is too small. It is evident that the amount of deionized water is also a crucial factor in controlling the leaching reaction. Even though water does not directly participate in the reaction, its role as a reaction medium influences the mass transfer process. Moreover, water dictates the solubility of not only the leached species, but also that of the leaching reagents. Additionally, since the solubility of each ion is constant at the same temperature, the quantity of water also affects the dissolution capacity of ions. Therefore, when the solution volume is too small, the leaching rate of lithium significantly decreases. Conversely, when the solution volume is small, the leaching rate of iron tends to increase. This is because the leaching rate of iron is originally low and far from reaching the solution's carrying capacity and diffusion control conditions. The reduction in deionized water leads to an increase in acid concentration and a decrease in pH, enhancing the leaching ability of the solution. Moreover, when the solution volume is too large, it increases wastewater discharge, demands larger reaction vessel sizes, and reduces the concentration of lithium ions in the leachate.

From Fig. 9, it can be concluded that, under the condition of a constant formic acid dosage (0.125 mol), using a solution volume of 50 mL to leach 2 g of lithium iron phosphate powder is the most reasonable.
Table 6 and Fig. 10 respectively reflect the experimental conditions and results of leaching under different temperature conditions.

As shown in the figure, increasing the temperature is beneficial for reducing the leaching rate of iron, while it has no effect on the leaching of lithium. It can be seen that the temperature rise is advantageous for the selectivity of lithium. This is because the elevated temperature favors the synthesis of iron phosphate. This indicates that under higher temperature conditions, iron and phosphate ions are more prone to bind, thereby further promoting the selectivity enhancement of leaching (Abd El-Lateef et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2017). When the temperature rises to 70 °C, the leaching rate of iron is only about 1.7 %, while the leaching rate of lithium remains above 99.9 %. This reflects excellent selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cost calculations and summary of oxygen and formic method
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we compared the cost of formic acid and acetic acid, two commonly used organic acids as lixiviants. According to information from the Alibaba website, the price of 85 % formic acid is 520 USD/ton (Alibaba, 2024a), while the price of 99.8 % acetic acid is 700 USD/ton (Alibaba, 2024b). Based on this research, the required amount of 85 % formic acid for processing one ton of waste lithium iron phosphate is around 64.5 L.
The required mass is 64.5L * 1.22 kg/L = 78.69 kg = 0.07869 ton.
The molar amount is 78.69 kg * 1000 *85% /46.03 g/mol = 1453.11 mol.
The cost is then calculated as 520 USD/ton * 0.07869 ton= 40.92 USD.
The required molar amount of formic acid is:1453.11 mol.
However, since the acidity of formic acid is ten times that of acetic acid, if acetic acid is used as the leaching agent, the approximate molar amount of acetic acid consumed would be:
That is approximately 14,531.1 mol*60.052 g/mol = 872,621.62 g = 0.873 ton
The consumption of 99.8 % acetic acid is then 0.873ton/99.8 % = 0.875 ton
Therefore, the cost would be 700USD/ton* 0.875ton = 612.5 USD
This calculation only considers the cost of the leaching agent and does not take into account factors such as volume changes due to acid density and wastewater treatment.

Considering that formic acid has the highest acid density and strongest acidity among organic acids, and as the carbon chain lengthens, acid density decreases and acidity weakens, it is meaningless to compare beyond formic acid and acetic acid. Therefore, here we only compared the costs of formic acid and acetic acid, and in comparison, the cost of using acetic acid is around 15 times that of formic acid. Formic acid is the most cost-effective organic acid as a leaching agent.

Here, XRD analysis (Fig. 11 ) was performed on the leaching residue under the optimal conditions, and it shows that its main component is iron phosphate, which is consistent with our previous theoretical analysis. This demonstrates an excellent lithium leaching rate and outstanding leaching selectivity.

According to Eqs. (2) and (3), calculations reveal that under optimal conditions, the distribution ratio of lithium between the solution and the residue is 379. This means that lithium in the solution is 379 times higher than in the residue. Correspondingly, the distribution ratio of iron between the solution and the leaching residue is only 0.0177, indicating a minimal leaching of iron, with the majority of iron remaining in the residue. Moreover, the separation coefficient between lithium and iron is as high as 21,404.44, demonstrating excellent leaching selectivity.

To further investigate the industrial feasibility of this method, we conducted experiments using air instead of oxygen as the oxidant. Since the oxygen content in air is approximately 21 %, we adjusted the air rate to be five times that used with oxygen. We maintained optimal conditions for leaching experiments, keeping the solution volume, liquid-to-solid ratio, formic acid concentration, and temperature constant. The leaching experiments were conducted under different time durations, as outlined in Table 7 , and the results are presented in Fig. 12 .

From Fig. 12, it is evident that by replacing oxygen with air as the oxidant, satisfactory lithium leaching rates and good leaching selectivity (Li > 97 %, Fe < 5 %) can still be achieved. However, the required time is longer. As shown in Fig. 12, the lithium leaching rate gradually increases with time, while the iron leaching rate shows a slight decreasing trend. When the reaction time is 6 h, satisfactory results are achieved with a lithium leaching rate of 97.81 % and an iron leaching rate of only 4.81 %. This demonstrates satisfactory selective leaching. Further extending the reaction time shows no significant changes in the leaching rates of lithium and iron. Therefore, we consider 6 h to be a reasonable reaction time.

In addressing the challenges of the widespread generation of waste lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries and the current low lithium recovery rates, this study has developed a novel, clean, low-cost, and sustainable method for lithium recovery from spent LiFePO4 batteries. This method replaces the traditional hydrogen peroxide with inexpensive and readily available oxygen, working synergistically with clean and non-polluting formic acid, achieving highly selective leaching of lithium from spent LiFePO4 powder. The approach avoids the drawbacks of hydrogen peroxide, such as storage difficulties, easy decomposition, vigorous reactions, strict reaction requirements, and susceptibility to splashing, thereby eliminating safety hazards, improving production efficiency, and reducing production costs. This paper, in conjunction with the E-pH diagram of the Li-Fe-P-H2O system, combines mechanistic discussions and experimental validation to verify the feasibility of this method. Experimental findings indicate that under the conditions of 2.5 mol/L formic acid concentration, 0.12 L/min oxygen flow rate, 25 mL/g liquid-to-solid ratio, 70 °C reaction temperature, and 3 h reaction time, the leaching rate of lithium can exceed 99.9 %, while the leaching rate of iron is only 1.7 %, demonstrating high lithium leaching efficiency and excellent leaching selectivity. Moreover, we also explored using air instead of oxygen as the oxidant and get the excellent lithium leaching rate (97.81 %) and low iron leaching rate (4.81 %), which shows the outstanding selectivity. This method introduces no harmful elements, using only raw materials containing C, H, and O as reaction agents, aligning with principles of clean production and sustainable development. Moreover, due to the ubiquity of oxygen, the method incurs only the raw material cost of formic acid. Additionally, as a selective leaching process with low formic acid consumption, it proves to be competitive in industrial applications, holding promising prospects. Although this method exhibited excellent leaching efficiency, selectivity, and demonstrated promising industrial prospects, there is still room for improvement in the future, as outlined below:
(1)
Special designs can be implemented for the oxygen supply equipment of the reactor, enabling simultaneous oxygen (or air) supply at multiple locations. This may result in a more uniform dissolution of oxygen during the reaction process, thereby enhancing the utilization efficiency of oxygen (or air).
(2)
High-pressure sealed reactors, such as autoclaves, could be utilized to increase the solubility of oxygen and reduce its release, thus saving on reagent costs.

Tianyu Zhao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Harshit Mahandra: Writing – review & editing. Yeonuk Choi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Weilun Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Zhifei Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Zhongwei Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Ailiang Chen: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
